author: @ebaab9913
text: To completely change the subject. I have an idea for a bicycle differential based on the Torsen diff. Really just a simplification. First the main housing and drive gear are combined, to be driven by the bicycle chain. So two multitoothed matching gears, about the size of a normal bicycle wheel hub gear, made of steel, have three (or two) tabs sets  bent out from the sides. Between those tabs goes the normal worm gears. The two gear plates are attached to each other with a pin in the centre which carries the two drive gears and shafts. Two bell housings with bearings for the driveshafts, are attached to the, main toothed gears. This is now a Torsen diff with the worm gears held by tabs each side of the driven bike cog. Need more info I will send a drawing. A symmetrical Torsen diff driven from a chain, and protected from dirt.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Sounds like an interesting idea for a bicycle differential - thanks for sharing!

author: @DM-kl4em
text: This is why it irritates me so much when people say "Tesla turbines have no torque".  They can spin at extremely high rpm, and you can turn all that speed into torque just by gearing them down.

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
That's the sort of ratio and torque which makes me think a precision printer could be made using it as a turning base so the print head then only has to move across the diameter and vertically, hence could be build more solidly and stable with no filament or wire tangle problems. A bonus is it'd mean less fatigue of the control and power feed wires to the printhead.

 The full diameter movement means the bottom plate would only ever have to rotate at max 180.0001 degrees,  halving the wear of components a full rotation design would have. A physical hard stop and accurate live positioning tracking mid print (re)calibration check should also be  possible, to be able to adjust to any motor/belt/whatever slippage.
Basically taking one axis of freedom of movement away from the printhead itself means it's mounting could be built with greater solidity, accuracy and tolerance of random ambient movement input, like jogging the bench the printer is sited on.

Downside is it might call for having to program a new slicing algorithm  but if so that's just a one off mathematical challenge, which even the cheapest modern processor can do very quickly, which I think could be incorporated into some onboard software, eg it took "normal" input data and transformed it into cylindrical polar co-ordinates, meaning software programs wouldn't have to build polar slicing into their products. Of course if such a device was produced commercially, if it's precision advantages were big enough that it became popular they soon would. Though I suspect building it onboard would be needed for the first few designs, to help it become popular enough that 3rd parties would put the effort in to support it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That's a really cool use case - cheers for sharing!

replies.author: @glencahalin4786
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Thanks, you're most welcome.

Another thing, that occurred to me overnight. 
Rotation and polar co-ordinates bring an almost infinitely precise positioning near the centre. Hence something like a multi nossle printhead ( switching between nossle positioning being just a co-ordinate translation, which is again something cheap computer chips can do rapidly ) would be possible. Positioning of where about the object was printed, on the turntable, could be exploited to utilise that. eg fairies dancing on a hammerhead, you'd print with the fairies at the centre, using the finest nossle, for precision, with the larger nossle doing the hammer for speed.

To be fair XY axis printer could easily use the multi-resolution print-head idea, for very little extra cost, but AFAIK don't seem too. Possibly I'm missing something about the, current, limits of things. My thinking is, being able to, appropriately, combine aspects of high resolution and speed seems such a good idea for their products I must be missing something as the why they don't already...Possibly components are only just hitting a price point where it'd make commercial sense to do so in the home printer market

author: @russellzauner
text: Ah nice, we're back here - this is how TQ broke through the ultralight mid drive market.  They patented a couple similar designs or design elements and they were ahead of the pack for probably 12-18 months until the nearest competitors finally got their footprint down and power up.  To me it seemed another novel design applied to a great use case like the NuVinci/Enviolo CVT.

author: @bryansmith2649
text: Clocks have cycloid gears.  They are good for transmitting high power into lots of movement, but not much power at the escapement end.  Involute gears in most machinery are good at transferring lots of movement (such as in a motor) into less motion and more power.

Seems like that kind of connection would be massively strong.

replies.text: I am not sure I understand but the plates do need to roll over the pins and progress - so i think that wouldn't be possible - but as I say I am not sure I really understand what it is you mean

replies.author: @GabrielPettier
replies.text: i believe you would lose the whole benefit of that setup, as it's the different number of pins that allow a slow (off center) rotation. you would be making a 1:1 gear box, though yes, much less at risk of ripping.

author: @grendel1960a
text: I once built a telescope drive using a bunch of planetary gearboxes from electric screwdrivers, I can see this style of gear working well as a telescope drive, how smooth is the output, would it give a smooth enough final drive to enable it to be used as a precision output to drive a telescope to track the stars at essentially 1 revolution per day, I wonder could you stack a third disc set driven by the second to slow the output even more?

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: so I calculate that using 4 disks, 39, 38, 37 and 36 and driving the input at around 1400 rpm gives you an output of 1 rev per day to drive a telescope

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: I'd make it using slanty rods instead of pins so they could be angled at different ways to provide different diameter end point which could be used for anti-jitter, driveshaft coupling alignment, and a smaller fractional subset of the original gear ratio like maybe 1500:1 to one moves to 1501:1 depending on whether you're pulling this "ring drive" into a larger or smaller diameter "ring" than the bases of the rods.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sure you could stack it mate and I am equally sure you would make a very nicely engineered version

replies.author: @newmonengineering
replies.text: If you look at some of the 3d printable designs of this already you can get some ideas for it. Some good designs even use small ball bearings instead of pins so to speak. When built well they are fairly quiet and have minimal backlash to them. But its always down to how good your tolerances are like everything else in engineering. There are designs that are kind of the opposite of this as well. I would definitely get some inspiration from existing 3d prints first because there are a tone of unique ideas around this gear system.

replies.author: @andrewch4066
replies.text:
Wherever in the world you are, you should be able to find a 3D printing lab in your city/country, send them the STL files, answer a few questions (which printing technology, material, color) and they will print for you. They should be able to send the parts to you by a delivery service
It is, of course, significantly more expensive than spent filament itself

Alternatively, you could find a hackerspace with a 3D printer that you could use
